findings.message: `prompt_template_path` points at `.harness/prompts/ref-verifier.md`, but that template file does not exist in the repository. Any code path that tries to render the ref-verifier prompt will fail closed at runtime, so the new capability is not actually usable yet.

The prompt contradicts its own fail criteria: after saying unrelated, contradictory, or over-broad claims should return `fail`, it then says that if the verifier cannot quote supporting text it should return `pending`. For the common deterministic-failure case where evidence does not support the claim, there is no supporting quote, so the model can classify real semantic mismatches as `pending` instead of `fail`. That routes fixable reference errors into the human-escalation path rather than the writer/fix loop, weakening the semantic gate.

The new `EvidenceCitation::try_new` API returns `ValidationError`, and this change adds a production `ValidationError` variant, but the T002 domain catalogue does not declare `ValidationError`; the generated `domain-type-signals.json` now contains a red `ValidationError` signal. The briefing explicitly treats non-add red/yellow domain type signals as factual findings, so the catalogue/implementation contract is inconsistent until `ValidationError` is declared/updated in the catalogue or the public signature avoids the undeclared type.

Missing `entry_hashes` entries are silently replaced with `ContentHash::from_bytes([0; 32])`. That fabricates a valid-looking but incorrect `entry_hash` in `<layer>-catalogue-spec-signals.json` if the adapter/key-derivation ever drifts, which should fail closed instead of persisting corrupted integrity metadata.

The new shared traversal helper is public and now drives both `catalogue_spec_refs` and `catalogue_spec_signals`, but the added tests only cover type entries. There is no direct coverage for trait/function traversal or for a same-short-name cross-section collision, so a regression in `section_key` formatting or section order would reach production unnoticed.

The merge gate now receives per-entry catalogue hashes, but it immediately discards `_entry_hashes` and only checks signal count, positional name, document-level `catalogue_declaration_hash`, and color. That means a committed `<layer>-catalogue-spec-signals.json` can contain a stale or fabricated `CatalogueSpecSignal.entry_hash` while the gate still approves, so the semantic Chain ② cache key no longer reflects the current catalogue entry. Compare each signal's `entry_hash()` against the current entry hash map using the same traversal/order and fail closed on missing or mismatched hashes.

`merge_gate.rs` now has 752 lines of production code before `#[cfg(test)]`, which exceeds the repo's 700-line maximum for production modules. This is an architecture violation and should be split into smaller modules.

The new Chain ② per-entry hash block pushes the production portion of `merge_gate.rs` to line 752 before `#[cfg(test)]`, exceeding the repository's 700-line maximum for production modules. This was 690 lines before the change, so the current patch crosses the guardrail; please split the new catalogue-spec integrity/signals logic into a helper module or otherwise reduce this file below the limit.

The cache merge is inverted: `retained` keeps existing entries whose `(claim_hash, evidence_hash)` do not match any current production pair, so cache-hit entries are dropped instead of preserved. A mixed hit/miss run will rewrite the cache without the hits, defeating AC-07 and forcing needless re-review on the next run.

Fast-tier evaluation is still fully sequential. `RefVerifyConfig::max_parallelism` is validated and stored, but `execute` never consults it; both fast passes use plain iterator collection (`.iter().map(...).collect()`). That misses the required parallel fast-tier execution / worker-cap behavior and leaves the configuration dead.

Any cache entry whose hashes match a current production pair is treated as a hit regardless of the stored verdict, so a cached `Fail`/`Pending` is never re-checked. The later merge at `639-669` then filters those same entries out of the rewritten artifact and never adds them back, so current cache hits can disappear entirely on the next save. That breaks AC-07 and can let stale failures vanish from the gate.

The return order is backwards for degraded calibration: `SemanticFailuresConfirmed` is emitted before `final_calibration_healthy` is checked. If known-bad detection stays below threshold and a production `Fail` is also confirmed, the degraded verifier's verdict is still trusted instead of escalating to `HumanEscalationRequired`, which contradicts the intended precedence.

As written, the fast/final verifier loops are strictly serial iterator chains, so the interactor never uses `RefVerifyParallelism` and can only issue one model call at a time. That makes the validated `max_parallelism` config dead and violates the requirement that fast-tier review be parallelized per pair.

`save_entries` runs before the final known-bad health check, so a batch that ends in `HumanEscalationRequired` still persists the final-tier verdicts from a degraded verifier into the verify-cache. The ADR/spec text says degraded-verifier verdicts are not trustworthy and should not be cached; gate cache writes on `final_calibration_healthy` (or defer persistence until after this check).
The production portion of this module is 767 lines long before the `#[cfg(test)]` block starts at line 770, which exceeds the repository's 700-line production-module limit.

The new public `RefVerifyApplicationService::execute` path is only covered on success-oriented scenarios; there is no test that stubs `RefVerifyPairSourcePort`, `RefVerifyVerifierPort`, or `RefVerifyCachePort` to return an error and asserts the corresponding fail-closed `RefVerifyError` variant is propagated. That leaves the main error-handling contract for this usecase unverified, which is a P1 coverage gap for a new public API.

The new public error variants `VerifierPort` and `CachePersistence` forward raw adapter `message` strings verbatim into `Display`, so any hostnames, SQL fragments, or stack traces returned by adapters will leak into user-visible output/logs. Redact or normalize these messages at the boundary instead of exposing the raw string.

The interactor test suite only covers success and behavioral cases. There are no tests that make `pair_source.load_pairs`, `cache.load_entries/save_entries`, or `verifier.verify_pair` return `Err`, so the new `VerifierPort` and `CachePersistence` failure paths are unverified. Add negative tests for each port boundary to satisfy the public-API test requirement.

On the first `Err` or panic from a worker, this returns immediately and drops the remaining `JoinHandle`s for the chunk. Those threads keep running detached, so a failed batch can still continue calling the verifier after the use case has already reported failure. Join or cancel the rest of the chunk before returning so the gate actually aborts fail-closed.

`HumanEscalationRequired { pair_count }` undercounts unresolved work when a degraded batch also has pending production pairs. The code returns either `confirmed_pending` or `known_bad_probes.len()`, so a run with both categories reports only one side of the problem instead of the total unresolved count described by the type docs.

`RefVerifyError::TrackNotActive` advertises an active-track guard, but `RefVerifyCommand` carries no current-branch/active-track input and this usecase layer has no branch-reader port. As written, nothing in this scope can actually enforce the promised guard, so the public API can run on a non-track branch without surfacing `TrackNotActive`.

The result classification gives `SemanticFailuresConfirmed` precedence over `HumanEscalationRequired`. If a batch contains both a confirmed production fail and at least one unresolved/pending pair, the function returns the fail variant and masks the pending case, even though the type docs say pending/unresolved results must surface as human escalation. This needs pending to win or a split result path.

`Pending` is checked before `Fail`, so a healthy batch that contains both verdicts returns `HumanEscalationRequired` instead of `SemanticFailuresConfirmed`. The intended rule is to prioritize confirmed failures in this mixed case so the writer/fix loop can act on them.

Cache retention is global across scopes: `fresh_hashes` is built without `cache_scope`, and the retain filter drops any existing entry whose `(claim_hash, evidence_hash)` appears in any freshly evaluated pair. If the same hash pair exists in another scope, saving this run will delete that scope's cache entry even though it was never re-evaluated. The pruning needs to be scope-local because caches are stored in separate artifacts per scope.

`AdrFrontMatterDto`/`AdrDecisionDto` only require `decisions[].id` and explicitly allow all other fields to be absent or unknown. As a result, an ADR front-matter block like `decisions: [{ id: D1 }]` or one with misspelled/unknown schema keys deserializes successfully, and any `adr_ref` to `D1` passes even though the ADR metadata is malformed. The briefing calls out typed-deserialization fail-closed behavior for unknown/missing fields, so this should either reuse the existing strict ADR front-matter codec or mirror its required fields/`deny_unknown_fields` behavior before accepting the anchor.

`build_catalogue_entry_hashes` keys the returned `HashMap` only by the bare entry name. Type and trait short names are not globally unique in a catalogue, so a same-named type/trait pair will overwrite one another here and later be assigned the wrong `entry_hash` when the refresher looks up hashes by the same bare key. This silently corrupts the generated `<layer>-catalogue-spec-signals.json` for valid catalogues that contain such collisions; the key needs to include the section (or another disambiguator).

`build_catalogue_entry_hashes` uses a flat `HashMap<String, ContentHash>` keyed only by the bare entry name, but `CatalogueDocument` only guarantees uniqueness within each of `types`, `traits`, and `functions`. A valid catalogue can therefore contain the same short name in different sections; this code will reject it as a collision and the refresher cannot distinguish the hashes. The key needs a section discriminator, or the structure needs to be nested by section, so cross-section name overlaps still round-trip correctly.

Validate `schema_version` before converting `layer` into a `LayerId`. As written, a future artifact with an unsupported schema version but a changed/invalid `layer` value will fail with `Validation` instead of `UnsupportedSchemaVersion`, which misclassifies the format error and breaks forward-compatibility behavior.

D7 states that concrete model values should live in `agent-profiles.json` and that non-canonical configuration examples should not be placed in the ADR body, but the preceding paragraph includes a concrete provider/model example (`claude-opus-4-7`, `gpt-5.4-mini`). Remove the concrete example or relax the stated rule; as written the ADR contradicts itself on whether model examples belong in the ADR.

T001 says to parse ADR front-matter with `serde_yaml::Value` and manually walk `decisions[].id`. That conflicts with the typed-deserialization rule for `libs/infrastructure/src/verify/`, which requires typed `Deserialize` DTOs instead of untyped Value walking in verify/guard code.

T003 makes `CatalogueSpecSignal.entry_hash` a required `ContentHash`, but T005 still requires decoding legacy `<layer>-catalogue-spec-signals.json` files without `entry_hash` and returning a `None`-equivalent. Those requirements cannot both be true as written; the codec needs either an explicit optional/migration wrapper or a non-optional field with a different legacy-handling rule.

T008 still permits a `todo!()`-style stub in `libs/infrastructure/src/ref_verify.rs`. That is a panic path in library code, which conflicts with the repo's no-panics rule and makes the adapter path unsafe if the stub is ever exercised.

The `RoundType` doc says `RefVerifierPort.verify_pair` takes `RoundType`, but `usecase-types.json` defines that port parameter as `domain::tddd::ModelTier`, and T008 explicitly says the adapter performs the `ModelTier -> RoundType` conversion. This catalogue entry is therefore stale and contradicts the layer boundary.

T008 makes the only planned `RefVerifierPort` implementation fail-closed after resolving provider/model, deferring the actual model invocation to a future stub. That contradicts the same plan's `ref-verify run`, cache-writing, and `check-approved` acceptance path, because no implementation task can ever produce `Pass` entries for the verify-cache artifacts.

`RefVerifierPort::verify_pair` returns a full `SemanticVerifyEntry` containing `claim_hash` and `evidence_hash`, but the port receives only raw `claim` and `evidence` strings plus a tier. IN-04 defines those hashes as externally determined identities, especially ADR git blob hash for Chain①, so the adapter cannot construct the required cache key correctly from this interface.

`CatalogueSpecSignal` is declared with a complete field list containing only `type_name`, `signal`, and `entry_hash`, but the spec and plan require the existing `catalogue_declaration_hash` to be retained and coexist with the new per-entry hash. Implementing this catalogue literally would remove the whole-file hash that CN-10 says must remain.

T007 requires the usecase layer to downgrade a citation-less Pass to Pending and even test that case, but the same artifacts make that state unrepresentable: `SemanticVerdict::Pass` requires `EvidenceCitation`, and `RefVerifierPort` returns `SemanticVerdict`. A citation-less pass cannot reach the interactor; this handling belongs in the adapter/codec boundary or should be removed from the usecase task.

T007 assigns `VerifySemanticRefsInteractor` responsibility for enumerating Chain pairs, reading existing verify-cache artifacts, skipping unchanged `(claim_hash, evidence_hash)` pairs, and writing updated cache artifacts, but the usecase catalogue only injects `Arc<dyn RefVerifierPort>`. There is no secondary port for spec/catalogue reads, cache persistence, hash source, or ref-verify config, so the plan either forces infrastructure I/O into the usecase layer or leaves the interactor unable to perform its stated workflow. Add explicit usecase ports/dependencies for these external operations or move the I/O responsibility out of the interactor plan.

The infrastructure catalogue declares `AgentRefVerifierAdapter::new` with only `profiles: Arc<AgentProfiles>`, while T008 says the adapter stores an agent execution client/runner and tests require `AgentRefVerifierAdapter::new` to receive both `AgentProfiles` and the runner. As written, the catalogue and implementation plan disagree on the adapter boundary, making the planned tests/implementation inconsistent. Include the runner/client dependency in the type catalogue constructor or revise T008 to explain how invocation is supplied without injection.

`RefVerifyError` documents `EscalationRequired` as human escalation, but the spec explicitly excludes immediate human escalation for known-bad-pair degradation and requires the D5 path to try the heavyweight model first before reporting to a human. T007 also says a below-threshold known-error detection test returns `EscalationRequired`, so an implementer following the catalogue would short-circuit the required段2 escalation path. Rename/scope the error to final human escalation only, or add a separate model-escalation state that preserves the D5 sequence.

T001 makes ADRs without YAML front matter skip anchor validation and pass, but AC-05 requires `plan-artifact-refs` to fail when `spec.json` contains an `AdrRef` with a non-existent anchor. With this exception, any anchored reference to a grandfathered ADR can never satisfy the stated existence check, so the plan can pass invalid anchors contrary to D3/AC-05. Either narrow AC-05 to front-matter ADRs or make missing front matter fail for anchored refs.

AC-06 says the per-entry hash must be added to catalogue entries and that the `<layer>-types.json` codec encodes/decodes it, but T003/T005 place `entry_hash` on `CatalogueSpecSignal` and update the `<layer>-catalogue-spec-signals.json` codec instead. That leaves the actual type catalogue entries without the hash AC-06 requires, so the implementation can complete the planned tasks while still not meeting the acceptance criterion for differential Chain② review.

T010 only adds `bin/sotp ref-verify check-approved` to the commit gate, while the spec requires the commit final gate to verify all Chains before commit and the plan defines `check-approved` as a cache-only reader. Without a commit-gate `bin/sotp ref-verify run` step, changed reference pairs after the phase gate are never semantically re-reviewed at the final gate, so the plan does not implement IN-02/D2's final verification step.

IN-11 requires both the public `/track:ref-verify` skill and the `bin/sotp ref-verify` subcommand, but the coverage map assigns IN-11 only to T007 and T009. T011 is the task that actually creates the `/track:ref-verify` skill and command file, so the current coverage lets IN-11 appear covered while omitting half of its required surface.

`CatalogueSpecSignal` declares only the updated `new` method here, but the implementation plan for T003 also requires an `entry_hash()` accessor and T005's tests consume that accessor. The type catalogue and plan therefore disagree on the public API needed for `entry_hash`, leaving implementers following the catalogue without the method required by the later codec task.

T007 skips unchanged `(claim_hash, evidence_hash)` pairs before fast-tier evaluation, but IN-08/AC-09 require known-bad pairs to be injected into each batch to monitor verifier degradation. As written, a stable known-bad probe with a cached entry is skipped, so later phase/commit runs can satisfy the cache rule without exercising the model or measuring detection rate. The plan needs to exempt known-bad probes from cache-hit skipping, or make them non-cacheable by design.

The cache port API cannot route results for `RefVerifyScope::All` into the required per-chain/per-layer cache artifacts. `save_entries(&cmd, Vec<SemanticVerifyEntry>)` receives a flat entry list, and each entry only carries hashes/verdict, while `All` covers Chain1 plus all Chain2 layers and IN-10/AC-03 require separate `spec-adr-verify-cache.json` and `<layer>-catalogue-spec-verify-cache.json` files. Without chain/layer identity on each pair/entry or explicit per-scope decomposition before saving, the commit-gate All scope is not executable.

`task-coverage.json` starts with `in_scope` mappings and has no `goal` section, so GO-01 through GO-03 are not mapped to any tasks even though the spec contains 43 elements including those goals. This leaves the top-level goals uncovered by the coverage artifact; add goal mappings or otherwise include GO-01/GO-02/GO-03 in task coverage.

IN-08 requires the known-bad injection rate and detection threshold to be configurable from a settings file with defaults, but task coverage maps IN-08 only to T007. T007 only introduces an injected `RefVerifyConfig` and explicitly keeps filesystem/config I/O out of the usecase; no CLI/composition task is assigned to load those settings or provide the 10%/90% defaults. As written, the plan cannot satisfy the spec's configurability requirement.

The spec requires `ref-verifier` to be independently configurable for model, timeout, and prompt, and AC-12 requires those settings not to interfere with `reviewer`. T006 only adds `provider`/`model`/`fast_provider`/`fast_model` and tests model resolution; T008 then formats the prompt in the adapter. There is no planned work for timeout or prompt configuration independence, so the plan only implements part of IN-09/AC-12.

T007 escalates only the known_bad probes when the fast-tier detection rate falls below threshold, then persists the production verdicts without rechecking the production pairs that were passed by the now-untrusted fast tier. That undercuts IN-08/AC-09's calibration gate: a batch-level monitor failure should cause the affected production work to enter the escalation path, otherwise the hard gate can cache false Pass verdicts from a verifier that just failed its known-bad check.

`AgentExecutionRunner` is declared as a `SecondaryPort` while its own docs and T008 describe it as an infrastructure-local runner abstraction used inside `AgentRefVerifierAdapter`. That places a hexagonal boundary port in the infrastructure layer for an adapter implementation detail; either make it a real usecase/domain port if it is part of the application boundary, or keep it as an infrastructure-internal trait and do not catalogue it as `SecondaryPort`.

T007's core flow says that when the known_bad detection rate falls below the threshold, the whole fast batch is re-run at final, including production pairs that already passed. That contradicts the same spec's IN-06/AC-08 requirement that only Fail/Pending pairs are escalated and that no batch-wide escalation occurs, while this task also claims to satisfy AC-08. As written, implementers cannot satisfy both the task and the acceptance criteria.

`RefVerifyConfig` is modeled as a value object but its percentage fields are plain `u8`s with no constructor or validated percentage type. T009 only says the CLI rejects out-of-range config, but this usecase-level config is injected directly and can still represent impossible states such as `known_bad_detection_threshold_percent = 255` or `max_parallelism = 0`, which can make the semantic gate fail or behave nonsensically outside the CLI path. The plan should make these settings validated at the type boundary, not only at one adapter.

T007 requires `RefVerifyPercent::try_new(0)` to succeed and uses the same percent type for both known-bad injection rate and detection threshold. That allows a config with 0% injection, so no known-bad probes are injected, or a 0% threshold, so degradation can never fall below the threshold. This contradicts IN-08/AC-09, which require known-bad probes to be injected into each batch and to trigger escalation when detection is below threshold. The plan should make the semantic health-check settings non-disableable, e.g. by validating injection and threshold as 1..=100 or by using separate field-specific value objects.

AC-08 states that only production pairs that Fail/Pending are escalated and that fast-pass pairs never run the final tier. That conflicts with the known_bad calibration path in AC-09/D5/D6: once the injected known_bad checks fall below threshold, the fast verifier for that batch has already been marked degraded, so caching same-batch fast-pass production verdicts without final re-evaluation (as T007 also specifies) can freeze false Passes despite the hard-gate intent.

`HumanEscalationRequired` is documented as the outcome when final-tier verification still has failed/pending production pairs. That collapses two D5 paths: verifier degradation/unresolved cases may require human escalation, but confirmed production failures are supposed to be handed back to the writer/fix loop and then reverified. As written, the usecase contract makes confirmed semantic failures look like human-escalation failures and does not represent the writer handback path.

T003 adds `entry_hash` to `CatalogueSpecSignal`, changes the constructor, and says all construction sites should pass the new value, but it explicitly defers `CatalogueSpecSignalsDocument` encode/decode changes to T005. Since the signals codec is one of the places that constructs/decodes `CatalogueSpecSignal`, the T003/T005 ordering leaves the plan unable to compile or verify at the T003 boundary. Merge the codec update into T003 or make T003/T005 a single executable task group.

T006/T008 require `timeout_seconds` and `prompt_template_path` to be added as typed fields on `CapabilityConfigDto` / `ResolvedExecution`, and say `resolve_execution("ref-verifier", ...)` returns those values. The infrastructure catalogue, however, only models `AgentProfiles` as a reference and keeps `resolve_execution` returning `Option<ResolvedExecution>` while exposing timeout/prompt as separate methods, without any `ResolvedExecution` or `CapabilityConfigDto` modification. That leaves the plan and catalogue with conflicting contracts for how the adapter obtains timeout/prompt, so the implementation can satisfy one artifact while violating the other.

IN-06 says only production pairs that Fail or Pending at the fast tier are escalated to final, but AC-08 requires fast-Pass production pairs in a degraded known_bad batch to be re-evaluated at final before caching. That leaves T007 with conflicting rules for the same path: either fast Passes are never escalated unless they Fail/Pending, or they must be escalated when calibration fails. The plan should state the known_bad degradation exception consistently in IN-06 or remove it from AC-08.

`RefVerifierPort::verify_pair` declares its tier parameter as `domain::tddd::ModelTier`, but the domain catalogue declares `ModelTier` under `tddd::semantic_verify` and the surrounding semantic types are referenced through `domain::tddd::semantic_verify::...`. This makes the cross-layer port contract inconsistent with the declared domain type catalogue and will steer T007/T008 toward a different or nonexistent type path instead of the dedicated semantic-review type.

The plan defines `RefVerifyPairSourcePort` and `RefVerifyCachePort` as infrastructure-backed ports for pair enumeration, known_bad injection, hash/cache routing, and artifact persistence, but the infrastructure catalogue only plans `AgentRefVerifierAdapter`. Since T007 delegates these responsibilities out of usecase and no concrete pair-source/cache adapters are planned, `bin/sotp ref-verify run` cannot be wired without adding unplanned infrastructure types/tasks.

T004 requires new verify-cache codec contracts (`SpecAdrVerifyCacheDocumentCodec::encode/decode`, `CatalogueSpecVerifyCacheDocumentCodec::encode/decode`, and DTO encode/decode behavior), but the infrastructure catalogue does not declare any codec type, function, or inherent impl for them. That leaves the AC-03 verify-cache codec work outside the catalogue/spec contract, so the plan artifacts are internally inconsistent: the implementation plan depends on contracts that the type catalogue cannot track or verify.

`SemanticVerdict` is treated as a 3-state enum (`Pass` / `Fail` / `Pending`) here and in the downstream codec/usecase/CLI tasks, but the briefing for this scope defines the domain type as only `Pass { citation }` / `Fail { reason }`. That is a design contradiction: either the briefing needs to be updated or the plan needs to remove `Pending` everywhere it propagates.

T002/T004 now describe `SemanticVerdict` as only `Pass`/`Fail` and omit the `pending` JSON form, while the domain type catalogue still declares a `Pending` variant for `SemanticVerdict` and documents it as the representation for inconclusive reviews. This leaves the implementation plan inconsistent with the catalogue it claims to implement: following the plan would produce domain/codecs that cannot satisfy the current catalogue contract or any cache entries using `Pending`. Either keep `Pending` throughout the plan/codecs or update the catalogue/spec artifacts in the same semantic change.

S6 now says only production `Fail` verdicts are promoted to the final tier, but the detailed T007 task body in the same artifact still requires both `Fail` and `Pending` to be promoted, matching ADR D5. Following this summary would leave healthy-calibration fast-tier `Pending` results without a final review path, making the later `final tierでも Pending` escalation condition unreachable for those pairs. The same summary text is duplicated in `impl-plan.json` line 139, so both artifacts need the same correction.

T009 defines `ref-verify check-approved` as reading only the verify-cache artifacts and returning success when all existing entries are `SemanticVerdict::Pass`. That does not establish that every current Chain pair has a Pass cache entry; an incomplete cache containing only Pass entries would pass even though T010/AC-04 require missing/unresolved pairs to block the commit gate. The plan needs an explicit completeness check or artifact marker so absent Pass cache for any current pair fails closed.

T002 is marked complete and its task text says it adds every new domain semantic-review type, including `EvidenceCitation`, but the scoped domain type catalogue still marks `EvidenceCitation` as yellow/unresolved. That leaves the plan internally inconsistent about whether the T002 slice is actually complete; either the catalogue signal should be blue for this required type or T002 should not be resolved yet.

`domain-types.md` now marks several T002-owned semantic verify domain types as blue (`ModelTier`, `SemanticVerdict`, cache documents, `SemanticVerifyEntry`), while `plan.md`/`impl-plan.json` still leave T002 as todo and describe those same types as work to be added. The artifacts therefore simultaneously claim this slice is implemented and still pending, which makes the plan state ambiguous.

`T002` is marked resolved (`[x]` in `plan.md`, `status: "done"` in `impl-plan.json`), but the task body still says `T002 は未完了として扱う`. That is a direct status contradiction across the two plan artifacts and makes the progress state ambiguous.

The S2 section still describes `EvidenceCitation` as the current yellow/unresolved work to complete, while the same task is now marked `[x]` as completed with a commit hash. That leaves the generated plan asserting both that T002 is done and that its only unresolved piece is still pending, which can mislead the next implementer about whether the domain value-object slice is complete. Update the S2 description to reflect the completed state, or keep T002 incomplete until that yellow state is actually resolved.

T001’s front-matter check is under-specified: `AdrFrontMatterDto { decisions: Vec<AdrDecisionDto> }` only validates `decisions[].id`, so malformed ADR front-matter with unknown/misspelled schema keys or missing required metadata can still deserialize and pass anchor validation. That contradicts the stated fail-closed typed-deserialization requirement.

`impl-plan.json`'s final validation task T012 still enumerates only AC-01..AC-12, but `spec.json` adds AC-13. As written, the plan never verifies the new fail-closed `MissingEntryHash` path, so the added acceptance criterion can be shipped unvalidated.

The new usecase-side artifacts introduced in `usecase-types.json` (`CatalogueEntryRef`, `iter_catalogue_entries`, `TrackBlobReader.read_catalogue_for_spec_ref_check`, and `RefreshCatalogueSpecSignalsError::MissingEntryHash`) do not have a corresponding implementation task in `impl-plan.json`; T003/T005 only cover the domain `CatalogueSpecSignal` and signal artifact regeneration. That leaves AC-13 without an executable implementation step.

`T003` / `S3` says `TrackBlobReader.read_type_catalogue` should be expanded to return `(CatalogueDocument, String, HashMap<String, ContentHash>)`, but the type registry and contract map put that 3-tuple on `read_catalogue_for_spec_ref_check` and leave `read_type_catalogue` as `(Vec<u8>, String)`. As written, the plan targets the wrong port and leaves the actual new API unchanged.

`AC-13` makes the missing-hash path key off `section_key`, but `RefreshCatalogueSpecSignalsError::MissingEntryHash` only carries `entry_key`. In the collision case that `section_key` was introduced to eliminate, the error cannot identify which entry actually missed a hash, so the fail-closed contract is not represented accurately.

`T003` names the failure as `RefreshCatalogueSpecSignalsError::MissingEntryHash { layer_id, entry_key }`, but the same task and the generated type catalogue define the payload field as `section_key`. That makes the error shape internally inconsistent and leaves the implementation target ambiguous.

S3’s last bullet says to extend `TrackBlobReader.read_type_catalogue`, but the rest of the plan/spec consistently attach the per-entry hash map to `read_catalogue_for_spec_ref_check`. `read_type_catalogue` is a different port shape, so this names the wrong method for the change.

T003 is internally inconsistent: it first requires adding an `entry_hash()` accessor to `CatalogueSpecSignal`, but later says the domain type definition adds only the `entry_hash` field and `new` constructor argument. That leaves the intended API surface ambiguous.

task-coverage.json assigns IN-13/IN-14 and AC-13 to T005, but T005's plan text only covers regenerating catalogue-spec-signals artifacts for entry_hash/AC-06 and does not mention section_key keying, iter_catalogue_entries, or MissingEntryHash fail-closed behavior. This is a semantic coverage contradiction: an implementer following T005 can leave the adapter-side section_key/hash-map requirements unimplemented while the coverage map still claims those requirements are handled.

T004 still describes the verify-cache codecs as encoding/decoding through `SpecAdrVerifyCacheDocumentDto` and `CatalogueSpecVerifyCacheDocumentDto`, but the same artifact set now removes all verify-cache DTO type entries from `infrastructure-types.json`, `infrastructure-types.md`, and the contract map. That leaves the plan artifacts contradictory about whether these DTOs are part of the infrastructure contract, and drops the only type-level contract for the pass/fail/pending JSON representation that T004 is supposed to implement. Restore the DTO entries or rewrite the codec contract so it no longer refers to named DTOs that the artifact set intentionally omits.

`T007` leaves the terminal outcome ambiguous when the same batch contains both a confirmed production `Fail` and a known_bad degradation / remaining `Pending` pair. The text says `HumanEscalationRequired` is returned when final-tier known_bad detection is still below threshold or `Pending` remains, but the next sentence says a confirmed production `Fail` returns `SemanticFailuresConfirmed`; there is no precedence rule for the overlap case.

T007 is internally inconsistent on degraded batches: the core step says any final-tier confirmed `Fail` should be saved to the verify-cache and return `SemanticFailuresConfirmed`, but the new priority rule says that when known_bad detection is still below threshold after the final tier, `HumanEscalationRequired` must take precedence because the verifier is untrusted. The plan does not say whether confirmed `Fail` verdicts in that case are cached or suppressed.

T008 says citation-missing `Pass` outputs may be handled as a `fail-closed verdict`, but T007 requires citation-missing `Pass` to be rejected as an error at the adapter/codec boundary and never reach the usecase. Those two behaviors are incompatible unless the plan picks one and states it explicitly.

`RefVerifyCacheScope` is introduced in T007/plan.md as a required usecase enum for cache routing, but this type catalog marks the same row red. In this repo's legend red means a contract violation, so the plan artifacts disagree on whether the type is valid and T007 is not semantically settled yet.

`T007` gives two incompatible outcomes for a healthy final-tier batch that contains both `Fail` and `Pending`: the first rule says `HumanEscalationRequired` is returned whenever final-tier `Pending` remains, but clause (b) later says the same mixed batch should save the `Fail` verdict and return `SemanticFailuresConfirmed`. The caller cannot determine whether mixed Fail/Pending results should escalate to humans or be handed back to the fix loop. Align this contract in both `impl-plan.json` and the mirrored text in `plan.md`.

The return-order policy is inconsistent: this summary says that if `Pending` remains after final tier, the result should be `HumanEscalationRequired`, but the task body later says a healthy batch with a confirmed `Fail` should still return `SemanticFailuresConfirmed` even if `Pending` also remains. Please make the precedence explicit and consistent across the section.

The T007 return rules are internally inconsistent: this block says any final-tier batch with remaining `Pending` should return `HumanEscalationRequired`, but it later says a healthy batch with a confirmed `Fail` should return `SemanticFailuresConfirmed` even when `Pending` also remains. Implementers cannot tell whether mixed `Fail`+`Pending` batches should escalate or confirm. The same wording is mirrored in `impl-plan.json`.

T007 is labeled `前提: なし (独立タスク)`, but the task body explicitly says completion depends on a separate type-designer update to `usecase-types.json` to add `Hash` for `RefVerifyCacheScope` and clear the red signal. That hidden cross-role dependency makes the task non-self-contained as written and the schedule cannot be executed exactly in the stated order.

T007 now states that if the final-tier known_bad detection rate remains below threshold, the verifier is degraded and suspicious Fail verdicts must not be saved, but the same task later instructs the implementation to save final-tier production verdicts from calibration-failure batches. That leaves the cache-persistence behavior undefined for the degraded-verifier case and could cause untrusted verdicts to be persisted instead of failing closed.

T007 still says the `RefVerifyCacheScope` type-signal is currently red and that type-designer must add `Hash` to `usecase-types.json` before T007 can complete, but this same change already adds that `Hash` requirement and the rendered catalogue now shows `RefVerifyCacheScope` as blue. This leaves the plan artifacts contradicting each other and will make the next implementer treat an already-resolved prerequisite as outstanding work. Update the T007 description in both plan artifacts to reflect that the Hash/type-signal prerequisite is resolved, or move the remaining completion condition to whatever work is actually still pending.
